methodology: Clio-style two-stage taxonomy (Tamkin, Handa, Durmus et al. 2024) applied to survey free-text. Stage 1: taxonomy proposed from reading all 330+288 responses. Stage 2: rule-based classifier with priority-ordered keyword matching assigns primary + secondary themes.

q49.question: In what specific areas in healthcare are women's health needs underrepresented in the data used for AI models?

taxonomy.definition: Menopausal and perimenopausal health — brain fog, HRT, post-menopausal symptoms, hormonal lifecycle gaps.
taxonomy.example_quote: So many symptoms related to menopause — weight gain, joint pain, elevated cholesterol — are not being treated as menopausal in nature. Instead, they are being treated independently, and those treatments are often ineffective.

taxonomy.definition: Cardiovascular disease in women — atypical MI/stroke presentations, women dismissed at ED with cardiac symptoms.
taxonomy.example_quote: I have a friend who is 42 just had a heart attack and was turned away because her symptoms as a woman were not indicative of an MI. She could have died.

taxonomy.definition: Reproductive-system conditions: endometriosis, PCOS, fibroids, ovarian cysts, pelvic pain.
taxonomy.example_quote: Anything related to women's pain management, PCOS and endometriosis simply due to poor understanding and therefore poor availability of good data.

taxonomy.definition: Fertility, pregnancy, postpartum health, maternal mental health, medication during pregnancy.
taxonomy.example_quote: I was shocked when I was pregnant at the lack of information from medication on pregnancy.

taxonomy.definition: Medications tested on male subjects, wrong doses for women, pharmacokinetic sex differences, drug labels.
taxonomy.example_quote: Pharmaceuticals, drugs researched and brought to market based on studies of male subjects traditionally.

taxonomy.definition: Historical underrepresentation of women in clinical trials; exclusion of pregnant women, postpartum women, and older women.
taxonomy.example_quote: Women's underrepresentation in clinical trials — both as Principal Investigators and as Patients.

taxonomy.definition: Symptoms dismissed as stress, cycle, psychological; women labeled complainers; doctors ignoring women's concerns.
taxonomy.example_quote: Doctors ignore women's comments regarding their health more than men. Women can be seen as complainers and neurotic.

taxonomy.definition: Autoimmune conditions (RA, lupus, MS, thyroid) — disproportionately female but underrepresented in training data.
taxonomy.example_quote: Autoimmune disorders, hormonal imbalance, bladder inflammatory, mental health.

taxonomy.definition: Pain management disparity — women report more pain but receive less effective treatment; fatigue not believed.
taxonomy.example_quote: Women report chronic pain more frequently than men, yet there's a data gap on pain perception and management in women, making AI models less effective at addressing this.

taxonomy.definition: Mental health, ADHD, autism, and neurodiversity — women historically under-diagnosed; intersection with women's hormonal cycles.
taxonomy.example_quote: Insufficient studies and data on the connection between various mood and behavioral disorders and women diagnosed with ADHD.

taxonomy.definition: Female-specific and female-biased cancers: ovarian, cervical, breast, endometrial.
taxonomy.example_quote: Ovarian cancer diagnosis.

taxonomy.definition: Intersectional gaps — women of color, mature women (>40), LMIC respondents, racial/ethnic stratification.
taxonomy.example_quote: Women of color and mature women over 40.

taxonomy.definition: Caregiver stress, working mothers' mental load, sandwich-generation labor, return-to-work from extended leave.
taxonomy.example_quote: Caregiving burden; tests designed by men for women (e.g., mammogram, pap smear); clinical data around pregnancy.

taxonomy.definition: Respondent asserts gaps exist across all areas — broad structural claim rather than specific condition.
taxonomy.example_quote: Across the board — limited publicly available data, limited R&D investment, antiquated medical practices, under-reporting.

taxonomy.definition: Symptoms present differently in women (general, not tied to a specific condition above). Most common generic response.
taxonomy.example_quote: Symptoms are not believed by treating physicians, and treatment options are limited to nonexistent.

taxonomy.definition: Treatment outcomes, patient-reported outcomes (PROs), preferences — the SurveyMonkey-provided examples respondents echo.
taxonomy.example_quote: Symptoms, treatment outcomes, patient reported outcomes, preferences.

taxonomy.definition: Diagnosis and diagnostic accuracy broadly — misdiagnosis, delayed diagnosis, one-size-fits-all diagnostic criteria.
taxonomy.example_quote: Symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.

taxonomy.definition: Treatment access, availability, affordability, insurance coverage — structural barriers distinct from condition-level data.
taxonomy.example_quote: Insurance coverage, time to receive treatment, treatment duration, QofL, symptoms and symptom management, access to specialists.

taxonomy.definition: Respondent unsure, unfamiliar with AI, or refuses to engage (including 'IDK', 'N/A', 'I don't think AI belongs in healthcare').
taxonomy.example_quote: I don't think AI models belong in healthcare at all.

q50.question: What steps do you think your organization could take to minimize gender bias in AI models used for healthcare decisions?

taxonomy.definition: Collect more and more representative health data, especially from women; expand datasets, fill gaps.
taxonomy.example_quote: Prioritize collecting more comprehensive and representative health data.

taxonomy.definition: Enroll more diverse populations in clinical trials — women, pregnant women, LMIC, racial diversity.
taxonomy.example_quote: Diversity in clinical trials, leveraging real world evidence data to train AI algorithms/models.

taxonomy.definition: Conduct regular bias audits, fairness standards, algorithmic monitoring; test models for bias before deployment.
taxonomy.example_quote: Conduct regular bias audits on AI models to identify and mitigate any potential gender biases.

taxonomy.definition: Hire more women into AI/tech/leadership roles; put women in decision-making positions about AI deployment.
taxonomy.example_quote: Include more women in decision making roles on how and which AI models to use; invest in updating health data, research, medical textbooks.

taxonomy.definition: Education, awareness, training, workshops about AI bias and women's health for employees, leaders, and clinicians.
taxonomy.example_quote: Discuss more about mental health with employees and work in collaboration for stress relief.

taxonomy.definition: Explicitly sex-stratify or sex-disaggregate data; build sex-specific models; disallow aggregation that hides sex differences.
taxonomy.example_quote: Develop gender-specific models; increase collaboration with healthcare experts.

taxonomy.definition: Keep humans in the loop; limit AI to advisory/informational role; don't let AI make clinical decisions autonomously.
taxonomy.example_quote: AI should not be making decisions; it can provide information, options.

taxonomy.definition: Regulatory or policy action — insurance coverage, standards, audits mandated externally.
taxonomy.example_quote: Demand insurance companies work on this issue — cover more tests.

taxonomy.definition: Respondent unaware of AI use, says it's not their company's problem, or expresses inability to answer.
taxonomy.example_quote: I am unsure if my organization uses AI models for healthcare decisions.
